title: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease – MCQ
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: The liver receives what percentage of total cardiac output?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 25% — The liver receives approximately 25% of cardiac output (~1500 mL/min). Of this, 75% comes from the portal vein and 25% from the hepatic artery.

questions.question: What percentage of hepatic blood supply comes from the portal vein?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 75% — The portal vein provides 75% of hepatic blood flow but only 50% of oxygen supply. The hepatic artery provides 25% of blood flow but 50% of oxygen supply.

questions.question: Child-Pugh classification assesses liver function based on all EXCEPT:

options.B: Serum albumin

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Serum creatinine — Child-Pugh score uses 5 parameters: Bilirubin, Albumin, Prothrombin time (INR), Ascites, and Encephalopathy grade. Creatinine is part of the MELD score, not Child-Pugh.

questions.question: Child-Pugh Class C (score 10–15) is associated with perioperative mortality of approximately:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 50% or higher — Child-Pugh C carries perioperative mortality >50% for major abdominal surgery. Class A: ~10%, Class B: ~30%, Class C: >50%.

questions.question: Which coagulation factor has the shortest half-life and is first to decrease in liver disease?

options.B: Factor V

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Factor VII — Factor VII has the shortest half-life (~6 hours) among coagulation factors and is the first to decline in liver disease. Its level is reflected by PT/INR.

questions.question: The most sensitive preoperative liver function test for synthetic function is:

options.B: Serum albumin

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Prothrombin time (PT/INR) — PT is the most sensitive indicator of hepatic synthetic function due to the short half-life of Factor VII. Albumin reflects chronic liver function (half-life 21 days).

questions.question: Serum albumin less than what value indicates severe liver disease and increased surgical risk?

options.B: 3.5 g/dL

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 3.0 g/dL — Albumin <3.0 g/dL indicates severe hepatic dysfunction. Low albumin causes reduced protein binding of drugs, increased free drug levels, and peripheral edema/ascites.

questions.question: In liver disease, which of the following is TRUE about drug protein binding?

options.A: Increased binding due to elevated albumin
options.B: Decreased binding leading to higher free drug fraction
options.D: Increased binding due to elevated globulin

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Decreased binding leading to higher free drug fraction — Hypoalbuminemia in liver disease results in reduced protein binding of drugs (thiopentone, diazepam), causing increased free drug levels and exaggerated clinical effects.

questions.question: Preoperative correction of coagulopathy in liver disease includes all EXCEPT:

options.B: Vitamin K injection

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Heparin infusion — Heparin would worsen coagulopathy. Treatment includes Vitamin K (if cholestatic), FFP for active bleeding, cryoprecipitate for low fibrinogen, and platelet transfusion if <50,000/μL.

questions.question: MELD score is calculated using which three parameters?

options.B: Bilirubin, Creatinine, INR

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Bilirubin, Creatinine, INR — MELD (Model for End-stage Liver Disease) uses serum bilirubin, creatinine, and INR. MELD >15 indicates significant surgical risk.

questions.question: In cirrhotic patients, preoperative ascites indicates:

options.B: Portal hypertension and increased surgical risk

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Portal hypertension and increased surgical risk — Ascites reflects portal hypertension and poor hepatic function. It impairs diaphragmatic excursion, increases aspiration risk, and is associated with higher perioperative mortality.

questions.question: Hepatic encephalopathy in the preoperative period is graded from:

options.B: Grade I to IV

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Grade I to IV — Grade I: altered mood/sleep; Grade II: drowsiness, inappropriate behavior; Grade III: stupor, confusion; Grade IV: coma. Surgery should be deferred in Grade III-IV.

questions.question: Preoperative optimization of hepatic disease patient includes all EXCEPT:

options.B: Treatment of encephalopathy with lactulose

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Starting high-dose NSAIDs for pain — NSAIDs impair renal function (hepatorenal risk), reduce platelet function, and may precipitate GI bleeding. They should be avoided in liver disease patients.

questions.question: Hyperdynamic circulation in cirrhosis is characterized by:

options.B: Low SVR, high cardiac output, low MAP

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Low SVR, high cardiac output, low MAP — Cirrhotic patients have splanchnic vasodilation (due to nitric oxide), reduced SVR, compensatory high cardiac output, and relatively low mean arterial pressure.

questions.question: Which volatile anesthetic agent is most preferred in liver disease?

options.B: Isoflurane

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Isoflurane — Isoflurane maintains hepatic arterial blood flow better than other agents. Halothane reduces hepatic blood flow the most and is associated with halothane hepatitis. Isoflurane and sevoflurane are preferred.

questions.question: Halothane is avoided in liver disease primarily because:

options.B: It reduces hepatic blood flow and can cause halothane hepatitis

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. It reduces hepatic blood flow and can cause halothane hepatitis — Halothane reduces hepatic arterial flow by 25-30% and is associated with immune-mediated fulminant hepatitis (1:35,000 adults). It is contraindicated in liver disease.

questions.question: Which muscle relaxant is the agent of choice in liver disease?

options.B: Vecuronium

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Atracurium/Cisatracurium — Atracurium undergoes Hofmann degradation (organ-independent) and ester hydrolysis. Its metabolism is independent of hepatic and renal function, making it ideal in liver disease.

questions.question: Succinylcholine duration may be prolonged in liver disease because:

options.B: Reduced plasma cholinesterase (pseudocholinesterase) synthesis

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Reduced plasma cholinesterase (pseudocholinesterase) synthesis — The liver synthesizes plasma cholinesterase. Severe liver disease reduces its production, prolonging succinylcholine block duration.

questions.question: Which opioid is preferred in liver disease due to minimal hepatic metabolism?

options.B: Fentanyl

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Remifentanil — Remifentanil is metabolized by tissue esterases (organ-independent). Morphine has prolonged action due to reduced glucuronidation. Fentanyl metabolism is also hepatic-dependent.

questions.question: Induction agent of choice in liver disease is:

options.B: Propofol in reduced dose

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Propofol in reduced dose — Propofol has both hepatic and extrahepatic metabolism. Dose should be reduced due to hypoalbuminemia (increased free fraction) and reduced hepatic clearance.

questions.question: Intraoperative monitoring in liver disease should include:

options.B: Invasive arterial BP, CVP, urine output, temperature, and coagulation

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Invasive arterial BP, CVP, urine output, temperature, and coagulation — Due to coagulopathy, hemodynamic instability, renal vulnerability, and hypothermia risk, comprehensive invasive monitoring is essential.

questions.question: Regional anesthesia in liver disease is relatively contraindicated when:

options.B: INR >1.5 or platelets <80,000

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. INR >1.5 or platelets <80,000 — Neuraxial techniques carry risk of epidural/spinal hematoma in coagulopathy. Generally contraindicated if INR >1.5, platelets <80,000, or active coagulopathy.

questions.question: Intraoperative hypoglycemia in liver disease occurs because:

options.B: Impaired hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Impaired hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis — The liver is the primary organ for maintaining blood glucose. In severe disease, depleted glycogen stores and impaired gluconeogenesis cause hypoglycemia.

questions.question: Intraoperative fluid management in liver disease should avoid:

options.B: Lactated Ringer's solution in severe liver disease

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Lactated Ringer's solution in severe liver disease — The diseased liver cannot metabolize lactate to bicarbonate efficiently. Lactate accumulation may worsen acidosis. Normal saline or balanced crystalloids without lactate are preferred.

questions.question: Which factor most decreases hepatic blood flow during anesthesia?

options.B: Adequate hydration

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: A. IPPV with high airway pressures — Positive pressure ventilation increases intrathoracic pressure, reducing venous return and thereby reducing portal venous flow. High PEEP worsens this effect.

questions.question: Factors that reduce hepatic blood flow intraoperatively include all EXCEPT:

options.B: High sympathetic stimulation

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Adequate intravascular volume — Maintaining intravascular volume preserves hepatic perfusion. Hypotension, sympathetic stimulation (surgical stress), IPPV, and vasoconstrictors all reduce hepatic blood flow.

questions.question: Temperature regulation in liver disease is impaired due to:

options.B: Reduced hepatic heat production and vasodilation

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Reduced hepatic heat production and vasodilation — The liver contributes significantly to heat production. Cirrhotic vasodilation increases heat loss. Active warming and temperature monitoring are essential.

questions.question: Benzodiazepines in liver disease have:

options.B: Prolonged duration due to reduced metabolism and increased free fraction

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Prolonged duration due to reduced metabolism and increased free fraction — Hepatic oxidation of benzodiazepines is impaired in liver disease. Combined with hypoalbuminemia, this causes prolonged and exaggerated sedation.

questions.question: Which benzodiazepine is relatively safer in liver disease due to glucuronidation (Phase II) metabolism?

options.B: Midazolam

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Oxazepam/Lorazepam — Oxazepam and lorazepam undergo Phase II conjugation (glucuronidation) which is relatively preserved in liver disease, unlike Phase I oxidation (diazepam, midazolam).

questions.question: The hepatic artery buffer response refers to:

options.A: Increase in portal vein flow when hepatic artery flow decreases
options.B: Compensatory increase in hepatic artery flow when portal venous flow decreases
options.C: Shunting of blood through portosystemic collaterals
options.D: Decrease in both hepatic artery and portal vein flow

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Compensatory increase in hepatic artery flow when portal venous flow decreases — This semi-reciprocal mechanism maintains total hepatic blood flow and oxygen delivery when portal flow is reduced.

questions.question: Intraoperative management of ascites includes:

options.B: Rapid paracentesis of all fluid at induction
options.C: Careful drainage with albumin replacement to prevent cardiovascular collapse

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Careful drainage with albumin replacement to prevent cardiovascular collapse — Large volume paracentesis (>5L) requires albumin replacement (6-8g per liter removed) to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction.

questions.question: Hepatorenal syndrome is characterized by:

options.B: Functional renal failure due to splanchnic vasodilation and renal vasoconstriction

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Functional renal failure due to splanchnic vasodilation and renal vasoconstriction — Hepatorenal syndrome is functional (kidneys are structurally normal). Splanchnic vasodilation causes underfilling, activating RAAS and causing renal vasoconstriction.

questions.question: Obstructive jaundice (biliary obstruction) is characterized by:

options.B: Elevated conjugated bilirubin, raised ALP, pale stools, dark urine
options.D: Elevated unconjugated bilirubin with normal ALP

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Elevated conjugated bilirubin, raised ALP, pale stools, dark urine — Biliary obstruction causes conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT, acholic stools, and choluria.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: In obstructive jaundice, Vitamin K deficiency occurs because:

options.A: Increased renal excretion of Vitamin K
options.B: Lack of bile salts prevents fat-soluble vitamin absorption from gut
options.C: Increased hepatic metabolism of Vitamin K

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Lack of bile salts prevents fat-soluble vitamin absorption from gut — Bile salts are essential for absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). Without bile in the gut, Vitamin K malabsorption causes coagulopathy.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: In obstructive jaundice, coagulopathy from Vitamin K deficiency responds to parenteral Vitamin K within:

options.B: 6-12 hours

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 24-48 hours — Parenteral Vitamin K corrects coagulopathy in obstructive jaundice within 24-48 hours. If PT does not correct with Vitamin K, it suggests hepatocellular damage rather than pure obstruction.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: The major renal risk in obstructive jaundice is:

options.B: Acute tubular necrosis (hepatorenal syndrome/bile nephropathy)

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Acute tubular necrosis (hepatorenal syndrome/bile nephropathy) — Obstructive jaundice predisposes to renal failure due to direct bilirubin toxicity to renal tubules, endotoxemia, and reduced renal perfusion.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Renal protection in obstructive jaundice includes:

options.B: Adequate preoperative hydration and maintaining urine output

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Adequate preoperative hydration and maintaining urine output — Preoperative IV fluids, maintaining urine output >0.5-1 mL/kg/hr, and avoiding nephrotoxic drugs are essential for renal protection in jaundiced patients.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Endotoxemia in obstructive jaundice occurs because:

options.B: Absence of bile in gut allows bacterial overgrowth and endotoxin absorption

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Absence of bile in gut allows bacterial overgrowth and endotoxin absorption — Bile salts normally prevent bacterial overgrowth and endotoxin absorption from the gut. Their absence leads to endotoxemia, predisposing to sepsis and renal failure.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Preoperative biliary drainage (PTBD/stenting) before surgery in obstructive jaundice is considered when:

options.B: Bilirubin >10 mg/dL with planned major surgery, to reduce complications

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Bilirubin >10 mg/dL with planned major surgery, to reduce complications — Preoperative biliary drainage may reduce renal complications, sepsis, and wound healing problems in severely jaundiced patients undergoing major procedures.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Anesthetic technique for patients with biliary obstruction preferably avoids:

options.B: Morphine and drugs causing sphincter of Oddi spasm

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Morphine and drugs causing sphincter of Oddi spasm — Morphine causes sphincter of Oddi spasm (up to 50-fold increase in biliary pressure). Fentanyl has less effect; glucagon or naloxone can reverse spasm if needed.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Which drug can relieve morphine-induced sphincter of Oddi spasm?

options.B: Glucagon

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Glucagon — Glucagon relaxes the sphincter of Oddi smooth muscle. Naloxone (opioid reversal), nitroglycerin, and volatile anesthetics also help relieve opioid-induced biliary spasm.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Pruritus in obstructive jaundice is caused by:

options.B: Deposition of bile salts in the skin

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Deposition of bile salts in the skin — Retained bile salts deposit in the skin and stimulate nerve endings causing intense pruritus. Cholestyramine (bile salt binder) and rifampin may provide relief.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Wound healing in obstructive jaundice is impaired due to:

options.B: Malnutrition, Vitamin K deficiency, and reduced immunity

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Malnutrition, Vitamin K deficiency, and reduced immunity — Obstructive jaundice impairs wound healing through protein malnutrition, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, impaired immune function, and endotoxemia.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Bradycardia is more common in jaundiced patients because:

options.B: Bile salts sensitize the myocardium to vagal stimulation

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Bile salts sensitize the myocardium to vagal stimulation — Elevated bile salts increase cardiac sensitivity to vagal tone and depress myocardial contractility. Atropine should be readily available.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: In obstructive jaundice, which antibiotic prophylaxis is important preoperatively?

options.B: Broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negative and anaerobic organisms

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negative and anaerobic organisms — Cholangitis is common; biliary organisms include E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, and anaerobes. Prophylaxis with piperacillin-tazobactam or similar is important.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Vecuronium and rocuronium should be used cautiously in liver disease because:

options.B: They undergo significant hepatic metabolism and biliary excretion

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. They undergo significant hepatic metabolism and biliary excretion — Vecuronium (40-60% biliary excretion) and rocuronium (hepatic uptake) have prolonged duration in liver disease. Atracurium/cisatracurium are preferred alternatives.

questions.question: Postoperative jaundice in previously healthy patient may be caused by all EXCEPT:

options.B: Halothane hepatitis

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: D. Use of atracurium — Atracurium does not affect the liver. Postoperative jaundice can result from blood transfusion hemolysis, drug-induced hepatitis (halothane), hepatic ischemia (hypotension), or sepsis.

questions.question: In biliary surgery, intraoperative cholangiography may produce false positive results if which drug has been given?

options.B: Morphine (sphincter of Oddi spasm mimics stone)

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Morphine (sphincter of Oddi spasm mimics stone) — Morphine-induced sphincter of Oddi spasm can prevent contrast flow into the duodenum, mimicking a common bile duct stone on cholangiography.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Mannitol is sometimes used in obstructive jaundice surgery to:

options.B: Maintain renal perfusion and urine output (renal protection)

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Maintain renal perfusion and urine output (renal protection) — Mannitol (osmotic diuretic) helps maintain renal tubular flow and prevent bile pigment-induced tubular damage in jaundiced patients.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Which is the safest volatile agent regarding hepatic blood flow preservation?

options.B: Enflurane

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Isoflurane/Sevoflurane — Isoflurane and sevoflurane best preserve hepatic arterial buffer response and total hepatic blood flow. Halothane causes the greatest reduction in hepatic blood flow.
